title: EA Operating Model
description: How the EA function organises, governs, and delivers architecture work — and what must change when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

overview.what: An EA Operating Model defines how the Enterprise Architecture function operates — not just what capabilities it has, but how decisions are made, how teams are structured, how work gets done, and how performance is measured. It is the blueprint for running EA as a service to the organisation.

dimensions.label: Capabilities
dimensions.description: What the EA function can do — the 30 core capabilities that define EA's service portfolio, from architecture vision development to compliance evaluation.

dimensions.label: Governance
dimensions.description: How architecture decisions are made and enforced — decision rights, review boards, compliance mechanisms, and escalation paths.

dimensions.label: People & Organisation
dimensions.description: Team structure, roles, competencies, and career paths — including new roles for AI governance and agent architecture.

dimensions.label: Processes
dimensions.description: How architecture work gets done — the development cycle, reviews, continuous architecture practices, and delivery methodology.

dimensions.label: Tools & Repository
dimensions.description: Platforms and content supporting EA — including new registries for models, agents, and machine-readable architecture data.

dimensions.label: Performance
dimensions.description: How EA effectiveness is measured — KPIs, maturity models, stakeholder satisfaction, and value contribution tracking.

operating_models.label: Centralised
operating_models.best_for: Regulated industries, early EA maturity, strong compliance needs
operating_models.description: A single, central EA team owns all architecture decisions, standards, and governance. Domain and solution architects report into or are closely governed by the central function. This model maximises consistency and control but can become a bottleneck at scale.
strengths: Maximum consistency in standards and architecture decisions across the enterprise
Clear accountability and ownership for architecture governance
Strongest compliance posture — all decisions go through a single review authority
Easiest model to establish for organisations building their first EA function
trade_offs: Can become a bottleneck as architecture demand grows
Risk of ivory tower perception if central team is disconnected from delivery
Slower decision-making for domain-specific questions that require deep business context
Limited scalability without significant headcount investment in the central team
operating_models.agentic_considerations: In the agentic organisation, centralised governance gains new importance for AI standards and agent deployment policies. However, the pace of AI change can overwhelm a purely centralised review process. Consider centralised policy, automated enforcement — where the central team defines rules as policy-as-code and compliance checking is automated in CI/CD pipelines.

operating_models.label: Federated
operating_models.best_for: Large enterprises, diverse business units, high domain autonomy
operating_models.description: Architecture capability is distributed across business units or domains, each with their own architects who operate with significant autonomy. A lightweight central team may set broad principles but does not control domain-level decisions. This model maximises speed and domain relevance but risks inconsistency.
strengths: Fastest decision-making — domain architects have authority to act without central approval
Deep business context — architects are embedded in their domains
Highly scalable as each domain manages its own architecture capacity
Greatest business satisfaction as EA is responsive and locally relevant
trade_offs: Risk of inconsistency across domains in standards, tooling, and architectural patterns
Difficult to maintain enterprise-wide views and cross-domain integration
Standards divergence can lead to integration challenges and technical debt
Harder to enforce compliance with enterprise-wide policies
operating_models.agentic_considerations: Federated models risk AI fragmentation — different domains selecting different models, protocols, and agent frameworks without coordination. For AI governance, this creates dangerous gaps in compliance, cost control, and risk management. At minimum, federated organisations need centralised AI standards and agent governance even if domain architecture decisions remain federated.

operating_models.label: Hybrid (Recommended)
operating_models.best_for: Most organisations, especially those deploying AI at scale
operating_models.description: Combines a central EA team that sets strategy, standards, and governance with federated domain architects who have autonomy within guardrails. The central team focuses on cross-cutting concerns, enterprise-wide views, and strategic alignment while domains handle domain-specific architecture. This is the most common and recommended model for organisations deploying AI at scale.
strengths: Balances consistency with speed — guardrails without bottlenecks
Central team can focus on strategic, cross-cutting, and AI-specific concerns
Domain architects maintain business context and delivery speed
Scalable through the federation while maintaining enterprise coherence
trade_offs: Requires clear role definitions to avoid confusion about decision rights
Coordination overhead between central and domain teams
Risk of gravitating toward one extreme (too centralised or too federated) without active governance
Needs mature communication and knowledge-sharing practices to function effectively
operating_models.agentic_considerations: The hybrid model is best suited for the agentic organisation. The central team governs AI standards, model portfolio, agent policies, and sovereignty compliance while domain architects design agent-augmented solutions within their business context. Policy-as-code enables the central team to enforce standards at scale without becoming a bottleneck. This mirrors the pattern emerging in leading organisations: centralise what must be consistent (AI governance, security, cost controls) and federate what benefits from domain expertise (agent design, use case prioritisation).

engagement.description: The engagement model defines when and how the EA function gets involved in projects, decisions, and change initiatives. Clear engagement rules prevent EA from being either a bottleneck or an afterthought.

tiers.label: Mandatory Review
criteria: New platform or infrastructure investment above threshold
Cross-domain integration or data sharing initiative
AI agent deployment or model onboarding
Strategic vendor selection or contract renewal
tiers.sla: Architecture review completed within 10 business days
tiers.agentic_addition: All AI agent deployments require mandatory architecture review regardless of project size, due to the cross-cutting risk and governance implications.

tiers.label: Recommended Consultation
criteria: Agent configuration or permission scope changes
tiers.agentic_addition: Agent permission scope changes and model substitutions should trigger consultation even if the project itself is below the mandatory threshold.

tiers.label: Self-Service Guidance
criteria: Standard technology stack selection within approved catalogue
Use of pre-approved agent templates and patterns
Routine model version updates within approved model family
tiers.sla: Guidance available on-demand via repository and knowledge base
tiers.agentic_addition: Pre-approved agent templates and model families enable teams to deploy within guardrails without EA review, enforced by automated compliance checks.

arb.scope: Enterprise-wide architecture direction, major platform decisions, AI strategy alignment
arb.members: CIO/CTO, Lead EA, Domain Leads, AI Platform Lead, CISO

arb.scope: Project architecture reviews, standard deviation requests, technology evaluations
arb.members: Lead EA, Domain Architects, Solution Architects, AI/Agent Architect

arb.scope: Domain-specific architecture decisions, cross-domain coordination, agent deployment reviews
arb.members: Domain Architects, Solution Architects, relevant SMEs

performance.description: EA must demonstrate its value through measurable outcomes. These KPIs track both traditional EA effectiveness and the new dimensions introduced by AI and agent governance.

kpis.label: Architecture Compliance Rate
kpis.description: Percentage of projects and deployments that pass architecture compliance checks. In the agentic organisation, this includes automated policy-as-code checks in CI/CD pipelines.

kpis.label: Time-to-Decision
kpis.description: Average elapsed time from architecture review request to decision. Self-service and automated compliance should handle 60%+ of requests instantly.

kpis.label: Stakeholder Satisfaction
kpis.description: Annual survey score from EA stakeholders measuring perceived value, responsiveness, and relevance. Must include new AI stakeholder groups.

kpis.label: Repository Adoption
kpis.description: Percentage of architecture data fields that are complete and current in the EA repository. Includes new AI registries (model, agent, sovereignty).

kpis.label: AI Governance Coverage
kpis.description: Percentage of deployed AI models and agents that are registered, governed, and monitored within EA frameworks. A new KPI specific to the agentic organisation.

maturity_model.label: Initial
maturity_model.description: Ad hoc EA activities. No formal processes, governance, or repository. Architecture decisions are made locally without coordination.
maturity_model.ai_readiness: Not ready for AI agent deployment. Must establish foundational governance first.

maturity_model.label: Developing
maturity_model.description: Basic EA processes emerging. Some standards defined. Architecture repository started but incomplete. Governance exists but is inconsistently applied.
maturity_model.ai_readiness: Can pilot individual AI use cases. Not ready for agent deployment or multi-model operations.

maturity_model.label: Defined
maturity_model.description: Standardised EA processes and governance. Repository actively maintained. Regular architecture reviews. Clear roles and responsibilities. Standards communicated and mostly followed.
maturity_model.ai_readiness: Ready for structured AI adoption. Can onboard agents with governance. Needs AI-specific standards and registries.

maturity_model.label: Managed
maturity_model.description: EA performance measured with KPIs. Continuous compliance monitoring. Architecture data is machine-readable. AI-specific governance is integrated. Policy-as-code is operational.
maturity_model.ai_readiness: Ready for scaled AI deployment. Agent lifecycle management, model portfolio governance, and AI FinOps are operational.

maturity_model.label: Optimising
maturity_model.description: Continuous improvement driven by data. EA is agent-augmented itself. Architecture decisions are informed by real-time data. Enterprise as Code is advancing. EA demonstrates measurable business value.
maturity_model.ai_readiness: Leading practice. EA function itself uses AI agents. Fully automated compliance. AI value progression management in place.
